codeSnapshot.label: smote.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE, BorderlineSMOTE, ADASYN, SVMSMOTE
from imblearn.combine import SMOTETomek, SMOTEENN
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline as ImbPipeline
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

X, y = df.drop('target', axis=1), df['target']
print('Class distribution:', y.value_counts().to_dict())
# {0: 9500, 1: 500}  → 19:1 imbalance

# ── 1. Basic SMOTE ──
smote = SMOTE(
    sampling_strategy=0.5,  # minority:majority ratio after oversampling
    k_neighbors=5,           # k nearest minority neighbours for interpolation
    random_state=42
)
X_res, y_res = smote.fit_resample(X, y)
print('After SMOTE:', pd.Series(y_res).value_counts().to_dict())
# {0: 9500, 1: 4750}  (sampling_strategy=0.5 → minority = 50% of majority)

# ── 2. Variants ──
# Borderline-SMOTE: only oversample minority instances near the decision boundary
border_smote = BorderlineSMOTE(kind='borderline-1', k_neighbors=5, random_state=42)

# ADASYN: adaptive — generates more synthetic samples in harder-to-learn regions
adasyn = ADASYN(sampling_strategy=0.5, n_neighbors=5, random_state=42)

# ── 3. Combination: SMOTE + cleaning ──
# SMOTETomek: oversample minority, then remove Tomek links (boundary noise)
smote_tomek = SMOTETomek(random_state=42)
X_res2, y_res2 = smote_tomek.fit_resample(X, y)

# ── 4. Correct: SMOTE inside CV (not before) ──
# WRONG: smote outside cross-validation leaks synthetic samples
# X_res, y_res = smote.fit_resample(X, y); cross_val_score(..., X_res, y_res)

# CORRECT: use imblearn Pipeline
pipeline = ImbPipeline([
    ('smote', SMOTE(sampling_strategy=0.5, random_state=42)),
    ('clf', GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42))
])
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
scores = cross_val_score(pipeline, X, y, cv=cv, scoring='f1')
print('CV F1:', scores.mean().round(3), '±', scores.std().round(3))

eli3: When one class is much rarer than the other (e.g., 1% fraud, 99% normal), a model can get 99% accuracy by predicting 'normal' for everything — it's learned nothing about fraud. SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) creates new synthetic examples of the minority class to help the model learn from it.

SMOTE doesn't duplicate existing minority examples (which would just reinforce the same points). Instead, it picks a minority example and one of its nearest neighbours, then creates a new point somewhere on the line between them. This populates the minority class's neighbourhood in feature space, helping the model draw better decision boundaries.

SMOTE is not always the right answer. For tree-based models (XGBoost, LightGBM), adjusting class weights is often simpler and equally effective. SMOTE is most beneficial for distance-based models (k-NN, SVM) and neural networks. And critically: SMOTE must only be applied to training data, never to validation or test data.

whenWhy.title: Fraud detection and rare event prediction
whenWhy.body: Class imbalance is the default in fraud detection (0.1–5% fraud rate), medical diagnosis (rare disease), and anomaly detection. Without intervention, most classifiers are biased toward the majority class. SMOTE (or class weighting) shifts the decision boundary toward better coverage of the minority class, improving recall for the rare event at some cost to precision.

whenWhy.title: Small minority class with distance-based models
whenWhy.body: k-NN and SVM are most sensitive to class imbalance because they make decisions based on local neighbourhood composition. A k-NN classifier in a 1:99 imbalanced dataset will almost always return the majority class for any query, since the k neighbours are predominantly majority-class. SMOTE increases the density of minority examples in feature space, improving neighbourhood composition.

whenWhy.title: When to avoid SMOTE
whenWhy.body: SMOTE is unnecessary or counterproductive when: (1) Using XGBoost/LightGBM with scale_pos_weight (class weights) — these models handle imbalance natively and efficiently. (2) The minority class is very small AND high-dimensional — SMOTE interpolates in d-dimensional space, and in high d the interpolated points may lie in low-probability regions. (3) The minority class forms isolated clusters — SMOTE generates points that connect clusters, creating unrealistic synthetic samples. Visualize the data with UMAP/t-SNE before applying SMOTE.

deepDiveIntro: SMOTE (Chawla et al., 2002) generates each synthetic minority sample by picking a real minority example, choosing one of its nearest minority-class neighbours at random, and placing the new point somewhere on the line segment between the two. This populates the minority class's region of feature space rather than simply duplicating existing points. The sampling strategy parameter controls the target ratio of minority to majority samples after oversampling. [1]

Borderline-SMOTE focuses oversampling on the minority examples that sit near the decision boundary — those whose neighbourhood is dominated by majority-class points. Samples deep inside the minority cluster are already well-represented and are left alone. ADASYN takes an adaptive approach: minority examples that are hardest to classify receive more synthetic neighbours, concentrating the oversampling budget where the model needs the most help. [2]

deepDive.heading: SMOTE Algorithm
deepDive.body: For minority class M with |M| = n_min samples. Target: generate N_syn = sampling_strategy × n_maj − n_min synthetic samples. For each minority sample xᵢ: compute k-NN among M. Randomly pick neighbour xₙₙ. Generate: x_syn = xᵢ + λ·(xₙₙ − xᵢ), λ ~ Uniform(0,1). Properties: (1) Synthetic points are convex combinations of real minority points — lie on line segments between nearest neighbours. (2) k_neighbors controls locality of interpolation: smaller k → more concentrated near existing samples, larger k → more spread. (3) For categorical features: SMOTE cannot interpolate (intermediate category values are meaningless). Solution: SMOTE-NC (Nominal-Continuous) selects the most frequent category value among the k neighbours for categorical features. [1]
deepDive.formula: x_syn = xᵢ + λ·(x_nn − xᵢ),   λ ~ Uniform(0,1)

deepDive.heading: SMOTE + Cleaning Combinations
deepDive.body: SMOTE alone may introduce noise: synthetic points near the class boundary may be mislabelled or in ambiguous regions. Cleaning methods remove noisy examples after oversampling. (1) Tomek Links: a Tomek link is a pair (x_min, x_maj) where each is the nearest neighbour of the other. Such pairs are borderline ambiguous. SMOTETomek removes the majority-class member of each Tomek link after oversampling, cleaning the boundary. (2) Edited Nearest Neighbours (ENN): removes any sample whose class label disagrees with the majority label among its k nearest neighbours. SMOTEENN applies SMOTE then ENN on both classes. These combinations typically improve classifier performance over SMOTE alone. [2]

deepDive.heading: The SMOTE CV Leakage Trap
deepDive.body: If SMOTE is applied before cross-validation, synthetic minority samples derived from training data contaminate the validation folds — the model is validated on points it effectively 'trained on' (or near). This inflates CV metrics. Correct approach: apply SMOTE inside each CV fold (only to the training portion). imblearn's Pipeline (not sklearn's Pipeline) correctly applies the resampler only during fit, not during transform. Always use imblearn.pipeline.Pipeline when using imbalanced-learn resamplers with cross-validation. [1]

takeaways: SMOTE: x_syn = xᵢ + λ·(x_nn − xᵢ), λ ~ Uniform(0,1). Interpolates between minority nearest neighbours. [1]
Borderline-SMOTE: oversample only DANGER region (near boundary). ADASYN: adaptive — more samples in harder-to-classify regions. [2]
SMOTETomek / SMOTEENN: combine oversampling with boundary cleaning. Better than SMOTE alone in most benchmarks. [2]
SMOTE must be applied inside CV (use imblearn.pipeline.Pipeline). Applying before CV inflates metrics via leakage. [1]
For tree models (XGBoost, LightGBM): scale_pos_weight or class_weight is usually simpler and equally effective. Use SMOTE for k-NN, SVM, neural networks. [1]

edgeCases.q: Does SMOTE work with mixed numeric and categorical features?
edgeCases.a: Standard SMOTE interpolates between feature vectors using Euclidean distance and linear interpolation — both meaningless for categorical features. SMOTE-NC (Nominal-Continuous) extends SMOTE: for categorical features, the synthetic value is selected as the mode (most frequent value) among the k nearest minority neighbours rather than interpolation. SMOTE-N handles purely nominal data using a distance metric based on value frequencies (Value Difference Metric). In practice: encode all categoricals as ordinal integers before applying SMOTE (imperfect but works empirically for tree models downstream), or use SMOTE-NC with explicit categorical column indices, or prefer class-weight balancing which has no interpolation step.

edgeCases.q: How much should you oversample? What is the optimal class ratio after SMOTE?
edgeCases.a: There is no universally optimal ratio — it depends on the dataset, model, and metric. Guidelines: (1) Start with sampling_strategy=0.1 (minority = 10% of majority) and compare to no resampling and to 0.5, 1.0 (balanced). Tune via cross-validation on the metric that matters (F1, PR-AUC). (2) Avoid going to fully balanced (1:1) for extreme imbalance (1:1000+) — the model then treats both classes as equally common at inference, causing massive over-prediction of the minority. A 1:5 or 1:10 ratio after resampling is often better than 1:1. (3) Combine with threshold tuning on the classifier's predict_proba output to find the operating point that matches the desired precision-recall trade-off on the real distribution.

sources.label: Chawla, N.V. et al. — SMOTE: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique, JAIR 16, 2002
He, H. et al. — ADASYN: Adaptive Synthetic Sampling Approach, IEEE IJCNN 2008
